Print_Text_DiaryPage1_info:
LATE JUNE, 1993

During the final days of June 1993, I was working as an intern at the Louisville State University.

It was an academic lab. Small, underfunded, filled with patched-together equipment and reagents nearing expiration.

Our team consisted of half a dozen researchers and three interns. We believed we were merely studying a possible zoonotic disease affecting local wildlife.

Simple things: altered immune responses, unusual leukocyte patterns, strange protein changes in some samples. 

 Nothing that justified alarm... except for the fetid smell lingering in the air for weeks.
Print_Text_DiaryPage2_info:
July 3, 1993

Rumors began circulating from St. Peregrin Hospital in Louisville. They spoke of fevers that would not break, strange skin necrosis, agitated patients, and quarantine protocols being overridden by orders “from above.”

It was around that time that Dr. Hughson, my direct supervisor, began receiving anonymous blood samples, always accompanied by small notes stamped with the St. Peregrin seal.

The strange part was that Hughson never made it clear who was sending them—let alone why everything arrived under such secrecy. He frequently disappeared, “accompanied by the military,” according to the campus reception desk. He claimed it was to “collect material” at a laboratory outside the University, but he never said where.

I found it all deeply confusing.
A civilian researcher accompanying the military?

At another laboratory no one knew the location of?

None of it made any sense.

My work was simple: catalog samples, operate centrifuges, observe leukocyte behavior, keep the laboratory diary, and organize reports.

I just followed orders.
Nothing out of the ordinary.
Print_Text_DiaryPage3_info:
July 4, 1993

Another unidentified sample arrived, like so many others. But when I analyzed the cellular structure, something became immediately clear: it was not human.

The proteins were wrong. The thermal response was aggressive. The blood cells… were not blood cells. I had never seen anything like it.

I called Dr. Hughson. He stood silently for a few seconds, staring at the slide, and then said:

“Record it as environmental contamination.”

And he left the room.
It convinced no one.

The team became divided: some believed it was a mutated zoonosis; others suspected a military leak. As for me, I honestly just recorded the data and tried not to think too much about it.
Print_Text_DiaryPage4_info:
July 6, 1993

One of the researchers disappeared. He simply never showed up for the morning shift. The day before, he had taken some samples home with him.

The next day, when we arrived at the laboratory, there was a military lockdown at the campus entrance. Helicopters circling overhead. Police officers demanding identification from every staff member.

We tried calling St. Peregrin.
“Temporarily unavailable.”

The press began reporting a mysterious “chemical fire in Knox County.”

A chemical fire?
Across the entire county?
And the army closing roads?

Who would believe that?

That was the moment I understood:
No one was going to protect us.
No one was going to warn us.
No one was going to save us.

And at the end of that day, I made the decision that changed everything:
I took every research document I could carry…
…and I ran.

I was not a scientist.
I was not military.
I was not a survivor.

I was just the intern.
But the only one who realized that if the government was surrounding the county, it wasn’t to protect the population. It was to hide what was really happening.
Print_Text_DiaryPage5_info:
July 8, 1993

On the eve of the disaster, everything finally made sense: the university lab was only studying part of the infection, the Memorial was already dealing with advanced cases, the county was compromised, and the so-called “chemical fire” was a lie.

I tried leaving the city, but I was stopped.
I tried escaping through back roads, but there were already roadblocks.
I ended up trapped here.

I carried with me a bag, a few tools, and every manual, note, and report I managed to salvage.
Print_Text_DiaryPage6_title: MY NOTES — 'How to Recreate the Campus Lab'
Print_Text_DiaryPage6_info:
July 9, 1993

I still don’t know how to do everything on my own.
But I learned a lot watching Dr. Hughson — and now, with the manuals I managed to save, I think I can try to rebuild part of the process.

1. First step: build the workbench.
Without it, I can’t do anything.

2. Then I need to construct the muffle furnace.
Because without glassware — flasks, test tubes, jars — nothing works.
Everything starts with glass.

Once I have enough tubes and flasks, I can finally assemble the other equipment.

6. Basic workflow I remember from the lab procedures:

COLLECTION
According to the manuals (and what I remember), the first thing I must do is:
I. collect fresh infected blood.
II. I need five tubes of it for the next step.

SEPARATION
With five tubes, I can use the centrifuge to separate them into:
a. plasma
b. blood cells

Plasma for vaccines.
Cells for leukocytes.

LEUKOCYTES
With the blood cells, at the microscope, I can isolate the leukocytes.
They are essential.

ANTIBODIES
Then, in the chromatograph (with the computer turned on), I can extract antibodies from the leukocytes.
This is what really matters.
Without them, there is no vaccine — and certainly no cure.

VACCINES
With plasma, leukocytes, and reagents, I can try to create:
I. simple vaccine
II. quality vaccine
III. advanced vaccine

But the cure...
The cure requires many antibodies.
Far more than I remembered.

I spent the whole night writing this. I hope it's worth something.
The sirens stopped. Maybe this is the end.

And now I'm alone.
No team, no real laboratory, no infinite supplies.

Just a bag, a few books, and the knowledge I stole before everything collapsed.

But I'll try.

Because someone has to...
